input.subject: Write Data Quality & Preprocessing section
input.description: Research and write a comprehensive section on data quality assessment and preprocessing strategies for ML. Cover: data profiling techniques, missing value strategies (imputation vs deletion vs flagging), outlier detection and treatment, data validation pipelines, class imbalance handling, data leakage prevention, train/test contamination. Include concrete code examples (Python/pseudocode), highlight high-impact vs incremental optimizations, and address common pitfalls. Format as markdown.

input.subject: Write Feature Engineering & Selection section
input.description: Research and write a comprehensive section on advanced feature engineering and selection. Cover: automated feature synthesis, domain-specific transformations, encoding strategies (target encoding, hashing, embeddings), temporal/interaction features, dimensionality reduction (PCA, UMAP, autoencoders), feature selection methods (mutual information, SHAP-based, recursive elimination, Boruta), feature stability analysis. Include code examples, high-impact vs incremental distinctions, pitfalls. Format as markdown.

input.subject: Write Model Architecture & Hyperparameter Tuning section
input.description: Research and write a comprehensive section covering two topics: (1) Model architecture matching to problem type - decision trees vs linear models vs neural networks vs gradient boosting, when to use what based on data size/structure/interpretability needs. (2) Systematic hyperparameter tuning - grid/random/Bayesian optimization, HyperBand/ASHA, population-based training, multi-fidelity methods, practical tuning workflows. Include decision flowcharts in text, code examples, and common pitfalls. Format as markdown.

input.subject: Write Validation, Ensembles & Regularization section
input.description: Research and write a comprehensive section covering three topics: (1) Robust validation and evaluation metric selection - CV strategies, stratification, time-series splits, metric selection by problem type, calibration-aware evaluation. (2) Ensemble and stacking techniques - bagging, boosting, stacking, blending, diversity metrics, weighted ensembles, practical ensemble pruning. (3) Regularization and bias-variance management - L1/L2/dropout/early stopping/data augmentation, diagnosing overfitting vs underfitting, learning curve analysis. Include code examples, high-impact vs incremental, pitfalls. Format as markdown.

input.subject: Write Post-Processing, Decision Framework & Pitfalls section
input.description: Research and write a comprehensive section covering: (1) Post-processing calibration - Platt scaling, isotonic regression, temperature scaling, conformal prediction, threshold optimization for specific metrics. (2) Decision criteria framework - how to prioritize techniques based on dataset size (small/medium/large), computational constraints, latency requirements, interpretability needs. Structure as a decision matrix/checklist. (3) Common pitfalls and diagnostics catalogue - data leakage, overfitting signs, distribution shift, feature importance instability, evaluation methodology errors, with specific diagnostic steps for each. Format as markdown.

input.name: data-quality-agent
input.prompt:
You are a research agent on team "ml-optimization-framework". Your task is Task #1: Write the Data Quality & Preprocessing section.

Research and write a comprehensive, actionable section (aim for 2000+ words) covering:

1. **Data Profiling & Assessment** - systematic approaches to understanding your data before modeling
2. **Missing Value Strategies** - imputation methods (mean/median/mode, KNN, iterative, deep learning), when to delete vs impute vs flag
3. **Outlier Detection & Treatment** - statistical methods (IQR, Z-score), isolation forest, DBSCAN, winsorization, when to keep outliers
4. **Data Validation Pipelines** - schema validation, distribution drift detection, Great Expectations / pandera style checks
5. **Class Imbalance Handling** - SMOTE, ADASYN, class weights, threshold adjustment, ensemble methods for imbalance
6. **Data Leakage Prevention** - target leakage, train-test contamination, temporal leakage, feature selection leakage
7. **Encoding Categorical Variables** - one-hot, ordinal, target encoding, hashing trick, learned embeddings

For each sub-topic:
- Mark as 🔴 HIGH-IMPACT or 🟡 INCREMENTAL
- Include a concrete Python code example or pseudocode
- List common pitfalls with diagnostic steps
- Note when the technique matters most (small vs large data, structured vs unstructured)

Write your output directly to the file: /teamspace/studios/this_studio/sections/01_data_quality.md

Use web search and Perplexity to find current best practices and recent developments (2024-2026). Mark your task complete when done.

input.name: feature-eng-agent
input.prompt:
You are a research agent on team "ml-optimization-framework". Your task is Task #5: Write the Feature Engineering & Selection section.

Research and write a comprehensive, actionable section (aim for 2000+ words) covering:

1. **Automated Feature Synthesis** - Featuretools, feature stores, automated interaction discovery
2. **Domain-Specific Transformations** - signal processing features, text features (TF-IDF, embeddings), time-series features (tsfresh), image features
3. **Encoding Strategies** - target encoding with regularization, hashing trick, learned entity embeddings, frequency encoding
4. **Temporal & Interaction Features** - lag features, rolling statistics, time since events, polynomial interactions, ratio features
5. **Dimensionality Reduction** - PCA, UMAP, t-SNE, autoencoders, randomized projections, when each is appropriate
6. **Feature Selection Methods** - filter methods (mutual information, chi-squared), wrapper methods (RFE, sequential), embedded methods (L1, tree importance), SHAP-based selection, Boruta, stability selection
7. **Feature Stability Analysis** - measuring feature importance consistency across splits, subsamples, and time

For each sub-topic:
- Mark as 🔴 HIGH-IMPACT or 🟡 INCREMENTAL
- Include a concrete Python code example or pseudocode
- List common pitfalls with diagnostic steps
- Note computational cost and scalability considerations

Write your output directly to the file: /teamspace/studios/this_studio/sections/02_feature_engineering.md

Use web search and Perplexity to find current best practices. Mark your task complete when done.

input.name: model-arch-agent
input.prompt:
You are a research agent on team "ml-optimization-framework". Your task is Task #2: Write the Model Architecture & Hyperparameter Tuning section.

Research and write a comprehensive, actionable section (aim for 2500+ words) covering:

**Part A: Model Architecture Matching**
1. **Decision Framework** - when to use linear/logistic regression vs tree-based vs gradient boosting vs neural networks vs other approaches
2. **Matching to Data Characteristics** - tabular data (gradient boosting advantage), images (CNNs/ViTs), text (transformers), time-series (TCN/LSTM/transformers), small data scenarios
3. **Interpretability-Accuracy Tradeoffs** - glass-box models (EBMs), inherently interpretable models, post-hoc explanation compatibility
4. **Computational & Latency Constraints** - model size vs accuracy Pareto frontier, edge deployment considerations, inference optimization (quantization, pruning, distillation)

**Part B: Systematic Hyperparameter Tuning**
1. **Search Strategies** - grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization (Gaussian processes, TPE), multi-fidelity methods
2. **Advanced Methods** - HyperBand, ASHA, population-based training (PBT), Bayesian optimization with GPUs (BOHB)
3. **Practical Workflows** - what to tune first, budget allocation, warm-starting, multi-objective optimization (accuracy vs latency)
4. **Tooling** - Optuna, Ray Tune, Weights & Biases sweeps, SigOpt, Bayesian Optimization library

For each sub-topic:
- Mark as 🔴 HIGH-IMPACT or 🟡 INCREMENTAL
- Include concrete code examples (especially Optuna/Ray Tune)
- Include a text-based decision flowchart for architecture selection
- List common pitfalls (e.g., tuning on test set, not budgeting compute)

Write your output directly to the file: /teamspace/studios/this_studio/sections/03_model_architecture_tuning.md

Use web search and Perplexity to find current best practices. Mark your task complete when done.

input.name: validation-agent
input.prompt:
You are a research agent on team "ml-optimization-framework". Your task is Task #4: Write the Validation, Ensembles & Regularization section.

Research and write a comprehensive, actionable section (aim for 2500+ words) covering:

**Part A: Robust Validation & Evaluation**
1. **Cross-Validation Strategies** - k-fold, stratified k-fold, group k-fold, time-series splits, nested CV for unbiased estimates, repeated CV
2. **Metric Selection** - accuracy/F1/AUC-ROC for classification, RMSE/MAE/MAPE for regression, domain-specific metrics, why accuracy is often misleading
3. **Calibration-Aware Evaluation** - Brier score, expected calibration error (ECE), reliability diagrams
4. **Statistical Significance** - proper comparison of models, paired t-tests, bootstrap confidence intervals, Bonferroni correction

**Part B: Ensemble & Stacking Techniques**
1. **Bagging** - Random Forests, bagged neural networks, variance reduction mechanics
2. **Boosting** - AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost, when boosting helps vs hurts
3. **Stacking & Blending** - meta-learner design, cross-validated predictions, diversity requirement, practical stacking architectures
4. **Ensemble Pruning** - when ensembles are too expensive, selecting a subset, Caruana's ensemble selection
5. **Diversity Metrics** - disagreement, double-fault, correlation-based, entropy-based diversity

**Part C: Regularization & Bias-Variance**
1. **Classical Regularization** - L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge), Elastic Net, when each helps
2. **Neural Network Regularization** - dropout, batch/layer normalization, weight decay, Mixup, CutMix, label smoothing
3. **Early Stopping** - proper validation set selection, patience tuning, restoring best weights
4. **Data Augmentation** - task-specific augmentation strategies, synthetic data generation
5. **Diagnosing Bias-Variance** - learning curves, train vs validation gap analysis, practical diagnostic flowchart

For each sub-topic:
- Mark as 🔴 HIGH-IMPACT or 🟡 INCREMENTAL
- Include concrete Python code examples
- List common pitfalls with diagnostic steps

Write your output directly to the file: /teamspace/studios/this_studio/sections/04_validation_ensembles_regularization.md

Use web search and Perplexity to find current best practices. Mark your task complete when done.

input.name: postprocess-agent
input.prompt:
You are a research agent on team "ml-optimization-framework". Your task is Task #3: Write the Post-Processing, Decision Framework & Pitfalls section.

Research and write a comprehensive, actionable section (aim for 2500+ words) covering:

**Part A: Post-Processing & Calibration**
1. **Probability Calibration** - Platt scaling, isotonic regression, temperature scaling (for neural nets), beta calibration
2. **Conformal Prediction** - distribution-free uncertainty quantification, coverage guarantees, practical implementation
3. **Threshold Optimization** - optimizing for F1, precision-recall tradeoff, cost-sensitive thresholds, Youden's J statistic
4. **Post-Processing for Fairness** - bias mitigation in predictions, equalized odds post-processing

**Part B: Decision Criteria Framework**
Create a structured decision framework (table/checklist format) for prioritizing techniques based on:
1. **Dataset Size** - small (<1K), medium (1K-100K), large (>100K)
2. **Computational Constraints** - limited GPU, single machine, cluster
3. **Latency Requirements** - real-time (<10ms), near-real-time (<100ms), batch
4. **Interpretability Needs** - black-box OK, need explanations, need inherently interpretable
5. **Problem Type** - classification, regression, ranking, anomaly detection

For each constraint combination, recommend specific techniques and their expected impact.

**Part C: Common Pitfalls & Diagnostics Catalogue**
Cover at least 10 common pitfalls with specific diagnostic steps:
1. Data leakage (target leakage, temporal leakage)
2. Look-ahead bias in time-series
3. Overfitting to validation set (too many rounds of tuning)
4. Distribution shift between train and production
5. Feature importance instability
6. Improper cross-validation for grouped/sequential data
7. Ignoring class imbalance in evaluation
8. Metric gaming (Goodhart's law)
9. Sampling bias in training data
10. Confounding variables

For each: describe the pitfall, how to detect it, and how to fix it.

Write your output directly to the file: /teamspace/studios/this_studio/sections/05_postprocessing_decision_framework.md

Use web search and Perplexity to find current best practices. Mark your task complete when done.

content.content:
1	# 4. Validation, Ensembles & Regularization
2	
3	This section covers robust validation strategies, ensemble methods, and regularization techniques that form the foundation of reliable machine learning systems. Proper validation ensures unbiased performance estimates, ensembles combine models for improved generalization, and regularization controls model complexity to prevent overfitting.
4	
5	## Part A: Robust Validation & Evaluation
6	
7	### 4.1 Cross-Validation Strategies
8	
9	#### 4.1.1 K-Fold Cross-Validation (HIGH-IMPACT)
10	
11	K-fold cross-validation is the workhorse of model evaluation. The dataset is partitioned into k equal folds, with each fold serving once as validation while the remaining k-1 folds form the training set.
12	
13	**When to use:** Most standard ML problems with i.i.d. data
14	
15	**Choosing k:**
16	- k=5: Good balance between computation and reliability
17	- k=10: Lower bias, more reliable estimates
18	- k=n (LOO): High variance, computationally expensive, rarely recommended
19	
20	```python
21	from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score
22	from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
23	import numpy as np
24	
25	X, y = load_data()  # Your data
26	model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
27	
28	# Basic 5-fold CV
29	kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
30	scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=kf, scoring='accuracy')
31	print(f"Accuracy: {scores.mean():.3f} (+/- {scores.std() * 2:.3f})")
32	```
33	
34	**Common Pitfall:** Not shuffling data when samples are ordered by class or time. Always use `shuffle=True` for i.i.d. data.
35	
36	#### 4.1.2 Stratified K-Fold (HIGH-IMPACT)
37	
38	Stratified k-fold preserves class distribution across folds—critical for imbalanced datasets where standard k-fold might create validation folds with no positive samples.
39	
40	```python
41	from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
42	
43	# For imbalanced classification (e.g., 95% negative, 5% positive)
44	skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
45	scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=skf, scoring='f1')
46	
47	# Check stratification worked
48	for train_idx, test_idx in skf.split(X, y):
49	    print(f"Train class ratio: {np.bincount(y[train_idx]) / len(train_idx)}")
50	    print(f"Test class ratio: {np.bincount(y[test_idx]) / len(test_idx)}")
51	```
52	
53	**Diagnosis:** If you see `undefined metric` warnings for ROC-AUC or F1, your validation folds lack positive classes—switch to stratified immediately.
54	
55	#### 4.1.3 Group K-Fold (HIGH-IMPACT)
56	
57	Group k-fold prevents data leakage when samples from the same group (patient, user, session) appear in both train and validation. Essential for:
58	
59	- Medical data: Multiple samples per patient
60	- User data: Multiple transactions per user
61	- Sensor data: Multiple readings from same device
62	
63	```python
64	from sklearn.model_selection import GroupKFold
65	import pandas as pd
66	
67	# Sample data with user_id column
68	df = pd.DataFrame({'features': X, 'target': y, 'user_id': user_ids})
69	groups = df['user_id'].values
70	
71	gkf = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)
72	for train_idx, test_idx in gkf.split(X, y, groups):
73	    train_groups = set(groups[train_idx])
74	    test_groups = set(groups[test_idx])
75	    assert len(train_groups & test_groups) == 0  # Verify no leakage
76	```
77	
78	**Common Pitfall:** Using standard k-fold on grouped data yields overconfident estimates—models learn group-specific patterns that don't generalize.
79	
80	#### 4.1.4 Time Series Cross-Validation (HIGH-IMPACT)
81	
82	Time series data requires temporal validation—never randomly shuffle. Use forward chaining where each validation fold comes after its training fold.
83	
84	```python
85	from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit
86	
87	# For temporal data (stock prices, sensor readings, etc.)
88	tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, test_size=100)  # Fixed test window
89	for train_idx, test_idx in tscv.split(X):
90	    assert test_idx[0] > train_idx[-1]  # Verify temporal order
91	
92	# Alternative: Expanding window
93	tscv_expanding = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, max_train_size=None)
94	
95	# Alternative: Sliding window (fixed-size training)
96	tscv_sliding = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, max_train_size=1000)
97	```
98	
99	**Common Pitfall:** Standard k-fold on time series creates look-ahead bias—model learns from future to predict past.
100	
101	#### 4.1.5 Nested Cross-Validation (HIGH-IMPACT)
102	
103	Nested CV provides unbiased performance estimates when tuning hyperparameters. Outer loop estimates performance; inner loop selects hyperparameters.
104	
105	```python
106	from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, GridSearchCV, cross_val_score
107	from sklearn.svm import SVC
108	
109	# Outer CV: Performance estimation
110	outer_cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
111	
112	# Inner CV: Hyperparameter tuning
113	inner_cv = KFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
114	
115	param_grid = {'C': [0.1, 1, 10, 100], 'gamma': ['scale', 'auto', 0.01, 0.1]}
116	
117	# Nested CV
118	outer_scores = []
119	for train_idx, test_idx in outer_cv.split(X, y):
120	    X_train, X_test = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]
121	    y_train, y_test = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]
122	
123	    # Inner loop: find best hyperparameters
124	    grid_search = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, cv=inner_cv, n_jobs=-1)
125	    grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
126	
127	    # Outer loop: evaluate with best params
128	    best_model = grid_search.best_estimator_
129	    outer_scores.append(best_model.score(X_test, y_test))
130	
131	print(f"Nested CV Score: {np.mean(outer_scores):.3f} (+/- {np.std(outer_scores):.3f})")
132	```
133	
134	**When to use:**
135	- Publishing research results
136	- Limited data (<10k samples)
137	- Need unbiased hyperparameter selection + performance estimate
138	
139	**Computational cost:** O(k_outer × k_inner × n_hyperparameters)
140	
141	#### 4.1.6 Repeated Cross-Validation (INCREMENTAL)
142	
143	Repeated k-fold reduces variance of performance estimates by repeating k-fold with different random splits.
144	
145	```python
146	from sklearn.model_selection import RepeatedKFold, RepeatedStratifiedKFold
147	
148	# Repeat 5-fold CV 3 times = 15 total fits
149	rkf = RepeatedKFold(n_splits=5, n_repeats=3, random_state=42)
150	rskf = RepeatedStratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, n_repeats=3, random_state=42)
151	
152	scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=rskf, scoring='accuracy')
153	print(f"Mean: {scores.mean():.3f}, Std: {scores.std():.3f}")
154	```
155	
156	**When to use:** Reporting results with confidence intervals where you need stable estimates.
157	
158	### 4.2 Metric Selection
159	
160	#### 4.2.1 Classification Metrics (HIGH-IMPACT)
161	
162	Accuracy is misleading for imbalanced data. Choose metrics aligned with your problem:
163	
164	```python
165	from sklearn.metrics import (
166	    accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score,
167	    roc_auc_score, average_precision_score, confusion_matrix,
168	    classification_report
169	)
170	
171	y_true, y_pred, y_proba = get_predictions()
172	
173	# For balanced classes
174	acc = accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred)
175	
176	# For imbalanced classes
177	precision = precision_score(y_true, y_pred)  # TP / (TP + FP)
178	recall = recall_score(y_true, y_pred)        # TP / (TP + FN)
179	f1 = f1_score(y_true, y_pred)                # Harmonic mean
180	
181	# For probability predictions
182	roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_true, y_proba)           # Threshold-independent
183	pr_auc = average_precision_score(y_true, y_proba)  # Better for imbalanced
184	
185	# Full diagnostic report
186	print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred))
187	
188	# Confusion matrix
189	cm = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred)
190	tn, fp, fn, tp = cm.ravel()
191	```
192	
193	**Metric Selection Guide:**
194	- Spam detection: Optimize precision (minimize false positives)
195	- Medical diagnosis: Optimize recall (minimize false negatives)
196	- Imbalanced binary: Use PR-AUC, not ROC-AUC
197	- Multi-class: Use macro/micro averaged F1 based on class importance
198	
199	#### 4.2.2 Regression Metrics (HIGH-IMPACT)
200	
201	```python
202	from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, mean_absolute_percentage_error
203	import numpy as np
204	
205	y_true, y_pred = get_predictions()
206	
207	# Scale-dependent errors
208	rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred))
209	mae = mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred)
210	mape = mean_absolute_percentage_error(y_true, y_pred)
211	
212	# Scale-independent (0-1, lower is better)
213	def max_error_normalized(y_true, y_pred):
214	    return np.max(np.abs(y_true - y_pred)) / (np.max(y_true) - np.min(y_true))
215	
216	# R-squared (explained variance)
217	from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
218	r2 = r2_score(y_true, y_pred)
219	```
220	
221	**When to use which:**
222	- RMSE: Penalizes large errors more heavily (sensitive to outliers)
223	- MAE: Robust to outliers, easier to interpret
224	- MAPE: Business-friendly percentage, but breaks at y=0
225	- R²: Communicative, but can be misleading for non-linear relationships
226	
227	#### 4.2.3 Custom Metrics (INCREMENTAL)
228	
229	Create domain-specific metrics when standard ones don't capture business value:
230	
231	```python
232	from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer
233	
234	# Example: Cost-sensitive metric for fraud detection
235	def business_cost(y_true, y_pred, cost_fp=10, cost_fn=1000):
236	    tp = np.sum((y_true == 1) & (y_pred == 1))
237	    fp = np.sum((y_true == 0) & (y_pred == 1))
238	    fn = np.sum((y_true == 1) & (y_pred == 0))
239	    return -(fp * cost_fp + fn * cost_fn)  # Negative for minimization
240	
241	business_scorer = make_scorer(business_cost, cost_fp=10, cost_fn=1000)
242	scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring=business_scorer)
243	```
244	
245	### 4.3 Calibration-Aware Evaluation
246	
247	#### 4.3.1 Brier Score & Expected Calibration Error (HIGH-IMPACT)
248	
249	A well-calibrated model's predicted probabilities match observed frequencies. If model predicts 0.8 for 100 samples, ~80 should be positive.
250	
251	```python
252	from sklearn.metrics import brier_score_loss
253	from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
254	import numpy as np
255	
256	# Brier score (lower is better, 0 is perfect)
257	brier = brier_score_loss(y_true, y_proba)
258	
259	# Expected Calibration Error
260	def expected_calibration_error(y_true, y_proba, n_bins=10):
261	    prob_true, prob_pred = calibration_curve(y_true, y_proba, n_bins=n_bins, strategy='uniform')
262	    bin_counts = np.histogram(y_proba, bins=n_bins, range=(0, 1))[0]
263	    bin_weights = bin_counts / len(y_true)
264	
265	    ece = np.sum(bin_weights * np.abs(prob_true - prob_pred))
266	    return ece
267	
268	ece = expected_calibration_error(y_true, y_proba)
269	
270	print(f"Brier Score: {brier:.4f}")
271	print(f"ECE: {ece:.4f}")
272	```
273	
274	**Diagnosing Calibration Issues:**
275	- Brier score decomposes into: calibration + refinement + uncertainty
276	- A lower Brier doesn't guarantee better calibration—model could have better discrimination
277	- Use ECE for direct calibration assessment
278	
279	#### 4.3.2 Reliability Diagrams (HIGH-IMPACT)
280	
281	Visual inspection of calibration:
282	
283	```python
284	from sklearn.calibration import CalibrationDisplay
285	import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
286	
287	# Single model
288	CalibrationDisplay.from_estimator(model, X_test, y_test, n_bins=10)
289	plt.show()
290	
291	# Multiple models comparison
292	fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
293	for name, model in models:
294	    CalibrationDisplay.from_estimator(model, X_test, y_test, n_bins=10, ax=ax, name=name)
295	plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', label='Perfect calibration')
296	plt.legend()
297	plt.show()
298	```
299	
300	**Common Calibration Patterns:**
301	- S-shaped curve: Under-confident (pushes probs toward 0.5)
302	- Inverse S: Over-confident (pushes probs toward 0 and 1)
303	- Random forest: Typically under-confident near extremes
304	- Naive Bayes: Typically over-confident
305	
306	#### 4.3.3 Calibration Methods (HIGH-IMPACT)
307	
308	```python
309	from sklearn.calibration import CalibratedClassifierCV
310	
311	# Sigmoid calibration (Platt scaling) - parametric, works well with small data
312	calibrated_sigmoid = CalibratedClassifierCV(
313	    base_estimator=model,
314	    method='sigmoid',
315	    cv='prefit'  # Use if model already fitted
316	)
317	calibrated_sigmoid.fit(X_cal, y_cal)
318	
319	# Isotonic regression - non-parametric, needs more data
320	calibrated_isotonic = CalibratedClassifierCV(
321	    base_estimator=model,
322	    method='isotonic',
323	    cv='prefit'
324	)
325	calibrated_isotonic.fit(X_cal, y_cal)
326	
327	# Temperature scaling - for neural networks, preserves ranking
328	# Requires external implementation (use torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss with T parameter)
329	```
330	
331	**Choosing a method:**
332	- Sigmoid: Small datasets (<1000 samples), symmetrical calibration error
333	- Isotonic: Larger datasets (>1000 samples), arbitrary monotonic distortion
334	- Temperature scaling: Deep networks, multi-class, preserves ranking
335	
336	**Important:** Always calibrate on held-out data, not training data. Use a dedicated calibration split or cross-validation.
337	
338	### 4.4 Statistical Significance Testing
339	
340	#### 4.4.1 Paired t-tests (INCREMENTAL)
341	
342	When comparing models, test if differences are statistically significant:
343	
344	```python
345	from scipy import stats
346	import numpy as np
347	
348	# Get cross-validation scores for both models (same folds!)
349	scores_model_a = cross_val_score(model_a, X, y, cv=10, scoring='accuracy')
350	scores_model_b = cross_val_score(model_b, X, y, cv=10, scoring='accuracy')
351	
352	# Paired t-test (same folds)
353	t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_rel(scores_model_a, scores_model_b)
354	
355	print(f"Model A: {scores_model_a.mean():.4f}")
356	print(f"Model B: {scores_model_b.mean():.4f}")
357	print(f"Difference: {scores_model_a.mean() - scores_model_b.mean():.4f}")
358	print(f"p-value: {p_value:.4f}")
359	
360	if p_value < 0.05:
361	    print("Significant difference (p < 0.05)")
362	else:
363	    print("Not significant - difference could be due to chance")
364	```
365	
366	**Critical:** Use paired tests on same folds. Unpaired tests are anti-conservative for CV comparisons.
367	
368	#### 4.4.2 Bootstrap Confidence Intervals (INCREMENTAL)
369	
370	Bootstrap provides non-parametric confidence intervals:
371	
372	```python
373	def bootstrap_ci(scores, n_bootstrap=10000, alpha=0.05):
374	    boot_means = []
375	    n = len(scores)
376	
377	    for _ in range(n_bootstrap):
378	        sample = np.random.choice(scores, size=n, replace=True)
379	        boot_means.append(sample.mean())
380	
381	    lower = np.percentile(boot_means, 100 * alpha / 2)
382	    upper = np.percentile(boot_means, 100 * (1 - alpha / 2))
383	    return np.mean(scores), lower, upper
384	
385	mean, lower, upper = bootstrap_ci(scores_model_b)
386	print(f"Mean: {mean:.4f}, 95% CI: [{lower:.4f}, {upper:.4f}]")
387	```
388	
389	#### 4.4.3 Multiple Comparison Correction (INCREMENTAL)
390	
391	When comparing many models, correct for multiple testing to avoid false positives:
392	
393	```python
394	from scipy.stats import spearmanr
395	from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests
396	
397	# Example: Comparing 10 models
398	p_values = [compare_model_i_to_baseline(i) for i in range(10)]
399	
400	# Bonferroni correction (conservative)
401	rejected, p_corrected, _, _ = multipletests(p_values, method='bonferroni')
402	
403	# Holm-Bonferroni (less conservative, more powerful)
404	rejected, p_corrected, _, _ = multipletests(p_values, method='holm')
405	
406	# Benjamini-Hochberg FDR (controls false discovery rate)
407	rejected, p_corrected, _, _ = multipletests(p_values, method='fdr_bh')
408	```
409	
410	**Common Pitfall:** Cherry-picking the best CV score without significance testing leads to inflated performance claims.
411	
412	---
413	
414	## Part B: Ensemble & Stacking Techniques
415	
416	### 4.5 Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)
417	
418	#### 4.5.1 Random Forests (HIGH-IMPACT)
419	
420	Random forests combine bagging with random feature selection to decorrelate trees:
421	
422	```python
423	from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, RandomForestRegressor
424	
425	# Classification
426	rf_clf = RandomForestClassifier(
427	    n_estimators=200,        # More trees = better, diminishing returns
428	    max_depth=None,          # Grow trees fully (low bias)
429	    min_samples_split=2,     # Regularization
430	    min_samples_leaf=1,
431	    max_features='sqrt',     # Random subset at each split
432	    bootstrap=True,          # Bagging
433	    oob_score=True,          # Out-of-bag estimation
434	    n_jobs=-1,
435	    random_state=42,
436	    class_weight='balanced'  # For imbalanced data
437	)
438	
439	# Regression
440	rf_reg = RandomForestRegressor(
441	    n_estimators=200,
442	    max_features=1.0/3,      # Recommendation for regression
443	    oob_score=True
444	)
445	
446	# OOB score is unbiased estimate (free validation set!)
447	rf_clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
448	print(f"OOB Score: {rf_clf.oob_score_:.4f}")
449	```
450	
451	**Variance Reduction Mechanism:**
452	- Bagging: Trains on bootstrap samples (sampling with replacement)
453	- Each tree sees ~63% unique samples, ~37% out-of-bag
454	- Averaging uncorrelated predictions reduces variance: Var(average) = Var/ntrees
455	
456	**Diagnosing Need for More Trees:** Plot OOB score vs n_estimators—should plateau.
457	
458	#### 4.5.2 Bagging Neural Networks (INCREMENTAL)
459	
460	```python
461	from sklearn.utils import resample
462	import numpy as np
463	
464	def bagged_nn_predictions(X_train, y_train, X_test, n_bags=10):
465	    predictions = []
466	
467	    for i in range(n_bags):
468	        # Bootstrap sample
469	        X_boot, y_boot = resample(X_train, y_train, replace=True, random_state=i)
470	
471	        # Train neural network
472	        model = create_nn_model()
473	        model.fit(X_boot, y_boot, epochs=100, verbose=0)
474	
475	        # Predict
476	        pred = model.predict(X_test)
477	        predictions.append(pred)
478	
479	    return np.mean(predictions, axis=0)
480	
481	# Reduces variance from neural network instability
482	```
483	
484	**When bagging helps:**
485	- High-variance models (deep trees, neural nets)
486	- Unstable learners (small data changes cause large prediction changes)
487	- Doesn't help high-bias models (linear models)
488	
489	### 4.6 Boosting
490	
491	#### 4.6.1 Gradient Boosting (HIGH-IMPACT)
492	
493	Boosting sequentially trains weak learners to correct previous errors:
494	
495	```python
496	from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier, GradientBoostingRegressor
497	from xgboost import XGBClassifier, XGBRegressor
498	from lightgbm import LGBMClassifier, LGBMRegressor
499	from catboost import CatBoostClassifier, CatBoostRegressor
500	
501	# Scikit-learn implementation
502	gb_clf = GradientBoostingClassifier(
503	    n_estimators=100,
504	    learning_rate=0.1,       # Shrinkage - smaller = more robust, needs more trees
505	    max_depth=3,             # Shallow trees for weak learners
506	    subsample=0.8,           # Stochastic gradient boosting (row sampling)
507	    min_samples_split=2,
508	    min_samples_leaf=1,
509	    random_state=42
510	)
511	
512	# XGBoost (faster, regularized)
513	xgb_clf = XGBClassifier(
514	    n_estimators=100,
515	    learning_rate=0.1,
516	    max_depth=3,
517	    subsample=0.8,
518	    colsample_bytree=0.8,    # Feature sampling
519	    reg_alpha=0.1,           # L1 regularization
520	    reg_lambda=1.0,          # L2 regularization
521	    eval_metric='logloss',
522	    early_stopping_rounds=10,
523	    random_state=42
524	)
525	
526	# LightGBM (efficient with large data)
527	lgbm_clf = LGBMClassifier(
528	    n_estimators=100,
529	    learning_rate=0.1,
530	    max_depth=3,
531	    num_leaves=31,           # LightGBM-specific: controls tree complexity
532	    subsample=0.8,
533	    colsample_bytree=0.8,
534	    reg_alpha=0.1,
535	    reg_lambda=1.0,
536	    verbose=-1
537	)
538	
539	# CatBoost (handles categorical features natively)
540	cat_clf = CatBoostClassifier(
541	    depth=6,
542	    learning_rate=0.1,
543	    iterations=100,
544	    l2_leaf_reg=3.0,
545	    random_seed=42,
546	    verbose=False
547	)
548	```
549	
550	**When boosting helps vs hurts:**
551	
552	| Scenario | Boosting | Bagging |
553	|----------|----------|---------|
554	| High bias (underfitting) | Helps | Limited help |
555	| High variance (overfitting) | Can overfit | Helps |
556	| Noisy data | Can overfit noise | More robust |
557	| Outliers | Sensitive | Less sensitive |
558	
559	**Common Pitfall:** Too high learning rate with too few trees—boosting overfits to noise. Use learning_rate=0.01-0.1 with n_estimators=200-1000.
560	
561	#### 4.6.2 AdaBoost (INCREMENTAL)
562	
563	```python
564	from sklearn.ensemble import AdaBoostClassifier
565	from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
566	
567	ada_clf = AdaBoostClassifier(
568	    base_estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1),  # Decision stumps
569	    n_estimators=50,
570	    learning_rate=1.0,
571	    algorithm='SAMME'  # For discrete AdaBoost
572	)
573	```
574	
575	**Use when:** Simple, interpretable weak learners (decision stumps) are sufficient. Generally outperformed by gradient boosting.
576	
577	### 4.7 Stacking & Blending
578	
579	#### 4.7.1 Stacking with Cross-Validated Predictions (HIGH-IMPACT)
580	
581	Stacking uses a meta-learner to combine base models. Critical: use out-of-fold predictions for training meta-learner to avoid data leakage.
582	
583	```python
584	from sklearn.ensemble import StackingClassifier
585	from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
586	from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
587	from sklearn.svm import SVC
588	from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_predict
589	import numpy as np
590	
591	# Define base models
592	estimators = [
593	    ('rf', RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)),
594	    ('gb', GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)),
595	    ('svm', SVC(probability=True, random_state=42))
596	]
597	
598	# Stacking with CV
599	stacking_clf = StackingClassifier(
600	    estimators=estimators,
601	    final_estimator=LogisticRegression(),  # Meta-learner
602	    cv=5,                                  # K-fold for OOF predictions
603	    stack_method='predict_proba',          # Use probabilities
604	    n_jobs=-1
605	)
606	
607	stacking_clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
608	
609	# Manual stacking for more control
610	def manual_stacking(X_train, y_train, X_test, base_models, meta_model, cv=5):
611	    from sklearn.model_selection import KFold
612	
613	    # Get OOF predictions for training meta-features
614	    kf = KFold(n_splits=cv, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
615	    meta_features_train = np.zeros((len(X_train), len(base_models)))
616	
617	    for i, (name, model) in enumerate(base_models):
618	        # OOF predictions via cross_val_predict
619	        oof_preds = cross_val_predict(model, X_train, y_train, cv=kf, method='predict_proba')
620	        meta_features_train[:, i] = oof_preds[:, 1]  # Positive class prob
621	
622	        # Retrain on full data for test predictions
623	        model.fit(X_train, y_train)
624	
625	    # Train meta-learner
626	    meta_model.fit(meta_features_train, y_train)
627	
628	    # Create meta-features for test set
629	    meta_features_test = np.zeros((len(X_test), len(base_models)))
630	    for i, (name, model) in enumerate(base_models):
631	        meta_features_test[:, i] = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
632	
633	    return meta_model.predict(meta_features_test)
634	```
635	
636	**Meta-learner design:**
637	- Simple models (LogisticRegression, RidgeRegression) often work best
638	- Regularized meta-learner prevents overfitting to base model predictions
639	- For regression: use linear/ridge regression
640	- For classification: use logistic regression or simple neural net
641	
642	#### 4.7.2 Blending (INCREMENTAL)
643	
644	Blending uses a holdout set for meta-learner training (simpler but wastes data):
645	
646	```python
647	def blend_models(X_train, y_train, X_test, base_models, meta_model, blend_size=0.2):
648	    from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
649	
650	    # Split training data
651	    X_tr, X_blend, y_tr, y_blend = train_test_split(
652	        X_train, y_train, test_size=blend_size, random_state=42
653	    )
654	
655	    # Train base models on main portion
656	    blend_features = []
657	    test_features = []
658	
659	    for name, model in base_models:
660	        model.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
661	        blend_features.append(model.predict_proba(X_blend)[:, 1])
662	        test_features.append(model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
663	
664	    # Train meta-learner on blend set
665	    meta_features = np.column_stack(blend_features)
666	    meta_model.fit(meta_features, y_blend)
667	
668	    # Predict on test
669	    test_meta = np.column_stack(test_features)
670	    return meta_model.predict(test_meta)
671	```
672	
673	#### 4.7.3 Practical Stacking Architecture (INCREMENTAL)
674	
675	```python
676	# Multi-level stacking
677	level_0 = [
678	    ('rf', RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100)),
679	    ('gb', GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100)),
680	    ('et', ExtraTreesClassifier(n_estimators=100))
681	]
682	
683	level_1 = [
684	    ('xgb', XGBClassifier(n_estimators=50)),
685	    ('lgbm', LGBMClassifier(n_estimators=50))
686	]
687	
688	# Level 0 predictions -> Level 1 -> Final prediction
689	from sklearn.ensemble import StackingClassifier
690	
691	stacking = StackingClassifier(
692	    estimators=level_0,
693	    final_estimator=StackingClassifier(
694	        estimators=level_1,
695	        final_estimator=LogisticRegression()
696	    ),
697	    cv=5
698	)
699	```
700	
701	**Diversity requirement:** Base models should make different errors. Correlated models provide little benefit over a single model.
702	
703	### 4.8 Ensemble Pruning
704	
705	#### 4.8.1 Ensemble Selection (INCREMENTAL)
706	
707	When ensembles are too expensive, select optimal subset:
708	
709	```python
710	from itertools import combinations
711	import numpy as np
712	
713	def ensemble_selection(models, X_val, y_val, n_select=None, metric='accuracy'):
714	    """Caruana's ensemble selection algorithm"""
715	    if n_select is None:
716	        n_select = len(models) // 2
717	
718	    # Get predictions from all models
719	    predictions = []
720	    for model in models:
721	        pred = model.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]
722	        predictions.append(pred)
723	
724	    predictions = np.array(predictions)
725	
726	    # Normalize predictions
727	    predictions = (predictions - predictions.mean()) / predictions.std()
728	
729	    # Greedy selection
730	    selected = []
731	    ensemble_pred = np.zeros(len(y_val))
732	
733	    for _ in range(n_select):
734	        best_score = -np.inf
735	        best_idx = None
736	
737	        for i in range(len(models)):
738	            if i in selected:
739	                continue
740	
741	            # Try adding this model
742	            test_pred = (ensemble_pred + predictions[i]) / (len(selected) + 1)
743	            score = compute_score(y_val, (test_pred > 0.5).astype(int), metric)
744	
745	            if score > best_score:
746	                best_score = score
747	                best_idx = i
748	
749	        if best_idx is not None:
750	            selected.append(best_idx)
751	            ensemble_pred += predictions[best_idx]
752	            ensemble_pred /= len(selected)
753	
754	    return [models[i] for i in selected]
755	```
756	
757	**When to prune:**
758	- Model size constraints (mobile deployment)
759	- Latency requirements (real-time prediction)
760	- Diminishing returns from additional models
761	
762	### 4.9 Diversity Metrics
763	
764	#### 4.9.1 Measuring Ensemble Diversity (INCREMENTAL)
765	
766	Diverse ensembles generalize better. Key metrics:
767	
768	```python
769	def disagreement_measure(y_true, preds_a, preds_b):
770	    """Fraction of examples where models disagree"""
771	    disagree = np.sum(preds_a != preds_b)
772	    return disagree / len(y_true)
773	
774	def double_fault_measure(y_true, preds_a, preds_b):
775	    """Fraction of examples where both models are wrong"""
776	    both_wrong = np.sum((preds_a != y_true) & (preds_b != y_true))
777	    return both_wrong / len(y_true)
778	
779	def q_statistics(y_true, preds_a, preds_b):
780	    """Correlation between model errors"""
781	    n00 = n01 = n10 = n11 = 0
782	
783	    for yt, pa, pb in zip(y_true, preds_a, preds_b):
784	        if pa == pb:
785	            if pa == yt:
786	                n11 += 1  # Both correct
787	            else:
788	                n00 += 1  # Both wrong
789	        else:
790	            if pa == yt:
791	                n10 += 1  # A correct, B wrong
792	            else:
793	                n01 += 1  # B correct, A wrong
794	
795	    if (n11 * n00 + n01 * n10) == 0:
796	        return 0
797	
798	    q = (n11 * n00 - n01 * n10) / (n11 * n00 + n01 * n10)
799	    return q  # Range: -1 (diverse) to 1 (similar)
800	
801	def entropy_diversity(predictions):
802	    """Average prediction entropy (for probabilistic predictions)"""
803	    # predictions: (n_models, n_samples, n_classes)
804	    mean_pred = predictions.mean(axis=0)  # Average prediction per sample
805	    entropy = -(mean_pred * np.log(mean_pred + 1e-10)).sum(axis=1)
806	    return entropy.mean()
807	
808	# Usage example
809	preds_a = model_a.predict(X_val)
810	preds_b = model_b.predict(X_val)
811	
812	disagreement = disagreement_measure(y_val, preds_a, preds_b)
813	double_fault = double_fault_measure(y_val, preds_a, preds_b)
814	q_stat = q_statistics(y_val, preds_a, preds_b)
815	
816	print(f"Disagreement: {disagreement:.3f}")
817	print(f"Double Fault: {double_fault:.3f}")
818	print(f"Q-statistic: {q_stat:.3f}")
819	```
820	
821	**Interpretation:**
822	- High disagreement: Models make different errors (good for ensembling)
823	- High double fault: Models fail on same examples (poor ensemble candidates)
824	- Q near 0: Independent errors (ideal)
825	- Q near 1: Correlated errors (little benefit from ensembling)
826	
827	---
828	
829	## Part C: Regularization & Bias-Variance
830	
831	### 4.10 Classical Regularization
832	
833	#### 4.10.1 L1 Regularization (Lasso) (HIGH-IMPACT)
834	
835	L1 regularization encourages sparsity—useful for feature selection:
836	
837	```python
838	from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso, LogisticRegression
839	from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel
840	
841	# Regression with L1
842	lasso = Lasso(alpha=0.1)  # alpha controls regularization strength
843	lasso.fit(X_train, y_train)
844	
845	# Check which features were selected
846	selected_features = np.where(lasso.coef_ != 0)[0]
847	print(f"Selected {len(selected_features)} out of {X.shape[1]} features")
848	
849	# Feature selection using L1
850	selector = SelectFromModel(LogisticRegression(penalty='l1', solver='saga', C=0.1))
851	selector.fit(X_train, y_train)
852	X_selected = selector.transform(X)
853	```
854	
855	**When L1 helps:**
856	- High-dimensional data with few relevant features
857	- Need interpretable models (sparse solutions)
858	- Feature selection as preprocessing step
859	
860	#### 4.10.2 L2 Regularization (Ridge) (HIGH-IMPACT)
861	
862	L2 regularization shrinks coefficients smoothly:
863	
864	```python
865	from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, LogisticRegression
866	
867	# Regression with L2
868	ridge = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
869	ridge.fit(X_train, y_train)
870	
871	# Classification with L2
872	log_reg_l2 = LogisticRegression(penalty='l2', C=1.0)  # C = 1/alpha
873	log_reg_l2.fit(X_train, y_train)
874	```
875	
876	**When L2 helps:**
877	- Multicollinear features (correlated predictors)
878	- Want all features but reduce overfitting
879	- Prefer stable coefficients over exact zeros
880	
881	#### 4.10.3 Elastic Net (INCREMENTAL)
882	
883	Combines L1 and L2 for balanced sparsity and stability:
884	
885	```python
886	from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNet, LogisticRegression
887	
888	# ElasticNet: alpha controls total strength, l1_ratio controls L1/L2 mix
889	# l1_ratio=0: pure L2, l1_ratio=1: pure L1, l1_ratio=0.5: equal mix
890	en = ElasticNet(alpha=1.0, l1_ratio=0.5, random_state=42)
891	en.fit(X_train, y_train)
892	
893	# For classification (use saga solver)
894	log_reg_en = LogisticRegression(
895	    penalty='elasticnet',
896	    solver='saga',
897	    l1_ratio=0.5,
898	    C=1.0
899	)
900	log_reg_en.fit(X_train, y_train)
901	```
902	
903	**When Elastic Net helps:**
904	- Correlated features where L1 arbitrarily selects one
905	- Want some sparsity but not as extreme as pure L1
906	- p >> n (more features than samples)
907	
908	### 4.11 Neural Network Regularization
909	
910	#### 4.11.1 Dropout (HIGH-IMPACT)
911	
912	Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training, preventing co-adaptation:
913	
914	```python
915	import torch
916	import torch.nn as nn
917	
918	class RegularizedNet(nn.Module):
919	    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_classes, dropout_rate=0.5):
920	        super().__init__()
921	        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden_size)
922	        self.dropout1 = nn.Dropout(dropout_rate)
923	        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, hidden_size)
924	        self.dropout2 = nn.Dropout(dropout_rate)
925	        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, num_classes)
926	
927	    def forward(self, x):
928	        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
929	        x = self.dropout1(x)  # Only active during training
930	        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))
931	        x = self.dropout2(x)
932	        x = self.fc3(x)
933	        return x
934	
935	# Dropout rate tuning
936	# - 0.2-0.3: Mild regularization
937	# - 0.5: Standard (original paper)
938	# - 0.7-0.8: Heavy regularization
939	model = RegularizedNet(input_size=784, hidden_size=256, num_classes=10, dropout_rate=0.5)
940	
941	# Important: Set model to eval mode during inference
942	model.eval()  # Disables dropout
943	with torch.no_grad():
944	    predictions = model(X_test)
945	
946	model.train()  # Re-enables dropout for training
947	```
948	
949	**Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to call `model.eval()` during inference—dropout stays active, causing stochastic predictions.
950	
951	#### 4.11.2 Batch Normalization (HIGH-IMPACT)
952	
953	Batch norm reduces internal covariate shift and provides regularization:
954	
955	```python
956	class BatchNormNet(nn.Module):
957	    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_classes):
958	        super().__init__()
959	        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden_size)
960	        self.bn1 = nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_size)
961	        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, hidden_size)
962	        self.bn2 = nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_size)
963	        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, num_classes)
964	
965	    def forward(self, x):
966	        x = self.fc1(x)
967	        x = self.bn1(x)
968	        x = torch.relu(x)
969	        x = self.fc2(x)
970	        x = self.bn2(x)
971	        x = torch.relu(x)
972	        x = self.fc3(x)
973	        return x
974	
975	# Batch norm tips:
976	# - Use before activation function
977	# - Can reduce/eliminate need for dropout
978	# - Adds noise during training (regularization effect)
979	# - Small batch sizes (<8) can make it unstable
980	```
981	
982	**Training vs Inference:**
983	- Training: Uses batch statistics (mean/var), adds noise
984	- Inference: Uses running statistics, deterministic
985	- Always call `model.eval()` for inference!
986	
987	#### 4.11.3 Weight Decay (HIGH-IMPACT)
988	
989	Weight decay is L2 regularization applied to network weights:
990	
991	```python
992	import torch.optim as optim
993	
994	# Standard weight decay
995	optimizer = optim.SGD(
996	    model.parameters(),
997	    lr=0.01,
998	    momentum=0.9,
999	    weight_decay=1e-4  # L2 regularization
1000	)
1001	
1002	# AdamW: Decoupled weight decay (recommended for transformers)
1003	optimizer_adamw = optim.AdamW(
1004	    model.parameters(),
1005	    lr=1e-4,
1006	    betas=(0.9, 0.999),
1007	    weight_decay=0.01  # True weight decay, not L2
1008	)
1009	
1010	# Different weight decay for different layers
1011	optimizer = optim.AdamW([
1012	    {'params': model.base.parameters(), 'weight_decay': 1e-2},
1013	    {'params': model.head.parameters(), 'weight_decay': 1e-4}
1014	], lr=1e-4)
1015	```
1016	
1017	**Weight decay values:**
1018	- 1e-4: Standard for CNNs
1019	- 1e-2 to 1e-1: For transformers and large networks
1020	- 0: No regularization (risky for large models)
1021	
1022	#### 4.11.4 Advanced Regularization Techniques (INCREMENTAL)
1023	
1024	```python
1025	# Mixup: Linear interpolation between samples
1026	def mixup_data(x, y, alpha=0.2):
1027	    """Returns mixed inputs, pairs of targets, and lambda"""
1028	    if alpha > 0:
1029	        lam = np.random.beta(alpha, alpha)
1030	    else:
1031	        lam = 1
1032	
1033	    batch_size = x.size()[0]
1034	    index = torch.randperm(batch_size)
1035	
1036	    mixed_x = lam * x + (1 - lam) * x[index, :]
1037	    y_a, y_b = y, y[index]
1038	    return mixed_x, y_a, y_b, lam
1039	
1040	# Mixup loss
1041	def mixup_criterion(criterion, pred, y_a, y_b, lam):
1042	    return lam * criterion(pred, y_a) + (1 - lam) * criterion(pred, y_b)
1043	
1044	# CutMix: Cut and paste between images
1045	def cutmix_data(x, y, alpha=1.0):
1046	    lam = np.random.beta(alpha, alpha)
1047	    batch_size = x.size()[0]
1048	    index = torch.randperm(batch_size)
1049	
1050	    # Get random bbox
1051	    W, H = x.size()[2], x.size()[3]
1052	    cut_rat = np.sqrt(1. - lam)
1053	    cut_w = int(W * cut_rat)
1054	    cut_h = int(H * cut_rat)
1055	
1056	    cx = np.random.randint(W)
1057	    cy = np.random.randint(H)
1058	
1059	    bbx1 = np.clip(cx - cut_w // 2, 0, W)
1060	    bby1 = np.clip(cy - cut_h // 2, 0, H)
1061	    bbx2 = np.clip(cx + cut_w // 2, 0, W)
1062	    bby2 = np.clip(cy + cut_h // 2, 0, H)
1063	
1064	    mixed_x = x.clone()
1065	    mixed_x[:, :, bbx1:bbx2, bby1:bby2] = x[index, :, bbx1:bbx2, bby1:bby2]
1066	
1067	    # Adjust lambda based on actual cut area
1068	    lam = 1 - ((bbx2 - bbx1) * (bby2 - bby1) / (W * H))
1069	    y_a, y_b = y, y[index]
1070	
1071	    return mixed_x, y_a, y_b, lam
1072	
1073	# Label smoothing
1074	class LabelSmoothingCrossEntropy(nn.Module):
1075	    def __init__(self, smoothing=0.1):
1076	        super().__init__()
1077	        self.smoothing = smoothing
1078	
1079	    def forward(self, pred, target):
1080	        n_classes = pred.size(-1)
1081	        log_preds = torch.log_softmax(pred, dim=-1)
1082	
1083	        # Smooth labels: (1 - eps) * one_hot + eps / n_classes
1084	        with torch.no_grad():
1085	            smooth_target = torch.zeros_like(pred)
1086	            smooth_target.fill_(self.smoothing / (n_classes - 1))
1087	            smooth_target.scatter_(1, target.unsqueeze(1), 1 - self.smoothing)
1088	
1089	        loss = torch.sum(-smooth_target * log_preds, dim=-1).mean()
1090	        return loss
1091	
1092	# Usage
1093	criterion_smooth = LabelSmoothingCrossEntropy(smoothing=0.1)
1094	```
1095	
1096	**When to use advanced augmentation:**
1097	- Mixup: General purpose, works for tabular too
1098	- CutMix: Image-specific, preserves spatial structure
1099	- Label smoothing: Prevents overconfident predictions, helpful for calibration

1101	### 4.12 Early Stopping
1102	
1103	#### 4.12.1 Proper Implementation (HIGH-IMPACT)
1104	
1105	Early stopping prevents overfitting by halting training when validation performance plateaus:
1106	
1107	```python
1108	import torch
1109	import numpy as np
1110	
1111	class EarlyStopping:
1112	    def __init__(self, patience=10, min_delta=0.001, restore_best_weights=True):
1113	        """
1114	        Args:
1115	            patience: How many epochs to wait after last improvement
1116	            min_delta: Minimum change to qualify as improvement
1117	            restore_best_weights: Whether to restore model from best epoch
1118	        """
1119	        self.patience = patience
1120	        self.min_delta = min_delta
1121	        self.restore_best_weights = restore_best_weights
1122	        self.best_score = None
1123	        self.counter = 0
1124	        self.best_weights = None
1125	        self.early_stop = False
1126	
1127	    def __call__(self, val_score, model):
1128	        if self.best_score is None:
1129	            self.best_score = val_score
1130	            self.save_checkpoint(model)
1131	        elif val_score < self.best_score + self.min_delta:
1132	            self.counter += 1
1133	            if self.counter >= self.patience:
1134	                self.early_stop = True
1135	                if self.restore_best_weights:
1136	                    model.load_state_dict(self.best_weights)
1137	        else:
1138	            self.best_score = val_score
1139	            self.save_checkpoint(model)
1140	            self.counter = 0
1141	
1142	        return self.early_stop
1143	
1144	    def save_checkpoint(self, model):
1145	        """Save model weights when we get a new best score"""
1146	        self.best_weights = model.state_dict().copy()
1147	
1148	# Training loop with early stopping
1149	def train_with_early_stopping(model, train_loader, val_loader, n_epochs=100):
1150	    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
1151	    optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters())
1152	
1153	    early_stopping = EarlyStopping(patience=10, min_delta=0.001)
1154	
1155	    train_losses = []
1156	    val_scores = []
1157	
1158	    for epoch in range(n_epochs):
1159	        # Training phase
1160	        model.train()
1161	        train_loss = 0.0
1162	        for X_batch, y_batch in train_loader:
1163	            optimizer.zero_grad()
1164	            outputs = model(X_batch)
1165	            loss = criterion(outputs, y_batch)
1166	            loss.backward()
1167	            optimizer.step()
1168	            train_loss += loss.item()
1169	
1170	        train_loss /= len(train_loader)
1171	        train_losses.append(train_loss)
1172	
1173	        # Validation phase
1174	        model.eval()
1175	        val_score = evaluate_model(model, val_loader)  # Higher is better
1176	        val_scores.append(val_score)
1177	
1178	        print(f'Epoch {epoch+1}/{n_epochs}, Train Loss: {train_loss:.4f}, Val Score: {val_score:.4f}')
1179	
1180	        # Check early stopping
1181	        if early_stopping(val_score, model):
1182	            print(f'Early stopping at epoch {epoch+1}')
1183	            break
1184	
1185	    return train_losses, val_scores
1186	
1187	def evaluate_model(model, data_loader):
1188	    """Return validation metric (higher is better)"""
1189	    model.eval()
1190	    correct = 0
1191	    total = 0
1192	    with torch.no_grad():
1193	        for X_batch, y_batch in data_loader:
1194	            outputs = model(X_batch)
1195	            _, predicted = torch.max(outputs.data, 1)
1196	            total += y_batch.size(0)
1197	            correct += (predicted == y_batch).sum().item()
1198	    return correct / total
1199	```
1200	
1201	#### 4.12.2 Patience Tuning (INCREMENTAL)
1202	
1203	```python
1204	# Patience guidelines:
1205	# - Small datasets (n < 1000): patience 5-10
1206	# - Medium datasets (1000 < n < 100k): patience 10-20
1207	# - Large datasets (n > 100k): patience 20-50
1208	
1209	# Adaptive patience (increase during training)
1210	class AdaptiveEarlyStopping(EarlyStopping):
1211	    def __init__(self, initial_patience=10, max_patience=50, **kwargs):
1212	        super().__init__(patience=initial_patience, **kwargs)
1213	        self.initial_patience = initial_patience
1214	        self.max_patience = max_patience
1215	        self.epoch = 0
1216	
1217	    def __call__(self, val_score, model):
1218	        self.epoch += 1
1219	
1220	        # Increase patience over time
1221	        new_patience = min(
1222	            self.initial_patience + self.epoch // 10,
1223	            self.max_patience
1224	        )
1225	        self.patience = int(new_patience)
1226	
1227	        return super().__call__(val_score, model)
1228	```
1229	
1230	**Common Pitfalls:**
1231	- Too small patience: Stops before convergence
1232	- Too large patience: Wastes computation, may overfit
1233	- Forgetting to restore best weights: Returns overfit model

1303	# Gaussian noise injection for regression
1304	def add_gaussian_noise(X, noise_level=0.01):
1305	    """Add small Gaussian noise to features"""
1306	    noise = np.random.normal(0, noise_level, X.shape)
1307	    return X + noise
1308	
1309	# Mixup for tabular
1310	def tabular_mixup(X, y, alpha=0.2):
1311	    lam = np.random.beta(alpha, alpha)
1312	    batch_size = X.shape[0]
1313	    index = np.random.permutation(batch_size)
1314	
1315	    mixed_X = lam * X + (1 - lam) * X[index]
1316	    mixed_y = lam * y + (1 - lam) * y[index]
1317	    return mixed_X, mixed_y

1320	### 4.14 Diagnosing Bias-Variance
1321	
1322	#### 4.14.1 Learning Curves (HIGH-IMPACT)
1323	
1324	Learning curves show how performance changes with training size—key diagnostic tool:
1325	
1326	```python
1327	import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
1328	from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve
1329	
1330	def plot_learning_curve(estimator, X, y, cv=5, train_sizes=None):
1331	    if train_sizes is None:
1332	        train_sizes = np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10)
1333	
1334	    train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
1335	        estimator, X, y, cv=cv, n_jobs=-1,
1336	        train_sizes=train_sizes,
1337	        scoring='accuracy',
1338	        shuffle=True,
1339	        random_state=42
1340	    )
1341	
1342	    train_mean = train_scores.mean(axis=1)
1343	    train_std = train_scores.std(axis=1)
1344	    val_mean = val_scores.mean(axis=1)
1345	    val_std = val_scores.std(axis=1)
1346	
1347	    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
1348	    plt.plot(train_sizes, train_mean, 'o-', label='Training score')
1349	    plt.fill_between(train_sizes, train_mean - train_std, train_mean + train_std, alpha=0.1)
1350	    plt.plot(train_sizes, val_mean, 'o-', label='Validation score')
1351	    plt.fill_between(train_sizes, val_mean - val_std, val_mean + val_std, alpha=0.1)
1352	
1353	    plt.xlabel('Training Set Size')
1354	    plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
1355	    plt.title('Learning Curve')
1356	    plt.legend(loc='best')
1357	    plt.grid(True)
1358	    plt.show()
1359	
1360	    # Diagnosis
1361	    final_train = train_mean[-1]
1362	    final_val = val_mean[-1]
1363	    gap = final_train - final_val
1364	
1365	    print(f"Final Training Score: {final_train:.4f}")
1366	    print(f"Final Validation Score: {final_val:.4f}")
1367	    print(f"Gap: {gap:.4f}")
1368	
1369	    if gap > 0.1:
1370	        if final_val < 0.8:
1371	            print("Diagnosis: High variance (overfitting) - gap is large")
1372	        else:
1373	            print("Diagnosis: Acceptable variance - model performs well")
1374	    elif final_val < 0.8:
1375	        print("Diagnosis: High bias (underfitting) - both scores low")
1376	    else:
1377	        print("Diagnosis: Good fit - model generalizes well")
1378	
1379	# Usage
1380	plot_learning_curve(RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100), X, y)
1381	```
1382	
1383	**Learning curve patterns:**
1384	- **High variance (overfitting):** Large train-val gap, validation plateaus
1385	  - Solution: More data, more regularization, simpler model
1386	- **High bias (underfitting):** Both scores low, close together
1387	  - Solution: More complex model, feature engineering, less regularization
1388	- **Good fit:** High scores, small gap, both converging

1446	    val_acc = accuracy_score(y_val, val_pred)

1459	    print(f"   Validation: {val_acc:.4f}")

1464	    print(f"   Validation: {val_f1:.4f}")
1465	
1466	    print(f"\n3. CALIBRATION (Brier, lower is better)")

1468	    print(f"   Validation: {val_brier:.4f}")
1469	
1470	    # Diagnosis
1471	    gap = train_acc - val_acc
1472	
1473	    print(f"\n4. DIAGNOSIS")
1474	
1475	    if gap > 0.15:
1476	        print("   HIGH VARIANCE (Overfitting)")

1478	        print("   Solutions:")

1482	        print("     * Use ensemble methods (bagging)")
1483	
1484	    elif val_acc < 0.7:

1486	        print("   - Both train and val scores low")
1487	        print("   Solutions:")
1488	        print("     * Increase model complexity")
1489	        print("     * Add more features")
1490	        print("     * Reduce regularization")
1491	        print("     * Try different model architecture")
1492	
1493	    elif gap < 0.05 and val_acc > 0.85:
1494	        print("   GOOD FIT")
1495	        print("   - Model generalizes well")
1496	        print("   Consider:")
1497	        print("     * Model is production-ready")
1498	        print("     * Monitor calibration for probability outputs")
1499	
1500	    else:
1501	        print("   MODERATE FIT")
1502	        print("   - Some room for improvement")
1503	        print("   Consider:")
1504	        print("     * Hyperparameter tuning")
1505	        print("     * Feature engineering")
1506	        print("     * Ensemble methods")
1507	
1508	    # Additional checks
1509	    print(f"\n5. ADDITIONAL CHECKS")
1510	
1511	    # Check for overfitting to calibration
1512	    if val_brier > train_brier * 1.5:
1513	        print("   - Calibration degrades on validation")
1514	        print("     Consider probability calibration")
1515	
1516	    # Check class balance issues
1517	    unique, counts = np.unique(y_train, return_counts=True)
1518	    if counts.min() / counts.max() < 0.1:
1519	        print("   - Severe class imbalance detected")
1520	        print("     Use stratified sampling, class weights, or appropriate metrics")
1521	
1522	    return {
1523	        'train_acc': train_acc,
1524	        'val_acc': val_acc,
1525	        'gap': gap,
1526	        'diagnosis': 'high_variance' if gap > 0.15 else 'high_bias' if val_acc < 0.7 else 'good_fit'
1527	    }
1528	
1529	# Usage
1530	diagnosis = diagnose_model(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, RandomForestClassifier())
1531	```
1532	
1533	---
1534	
1535	## Summary Checklist
1536	
1537	### Validation & Evaluation
1538	- [ ] Use stratified k-fold for imbalanced classification
1539	- [ ] Use group k-fold when samples share group IDs
1540	- [ ] Use time series split for temporal data
1541	- [ ] Apply nested CV for unbiased hyperparameter selection
1542	- [ ] Choose metrics aligned with business objectives
1543	- [ ] Check calibration for probability predictions
1544	- [ ] Use statistical tests for model comparisons
1545	
1546	### Ensembles
1547	- [ ] Try bagging (Random Forest) for high-variance models
1548	- [ ] Try boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM) for high-bias problems
1549	- [ ] Use stacking with diverse base models
1550	- [ ] Verify base models have diverse errors
1551	- [ ] Consider ensemble pruning for deployment constraints
1552	
1553	### Regularization
1554	- [ ] Apply L1 for feature selection, L2 for stability
1555	- [ ] Use dropout (0.3-0.5) for neural networks
1556	- [ ] Apply batch normalization before activations
1557	- [ ] Implement early stopping with patience tuning
1558	- [ ] Use data augmentation appropriate to your domain
1559	- [ ] Diagnose bias-variance with learning curves
1560
